experiments.title: How the placement of broadcasts impacts clicks on a ‘watch now’ page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the layout for potential donors will achieve a change in clicks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2025 - 04/08/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go to watch past sermons and TV broadcasts.
Building upon this previous experiment where we tested a grid layout, this next iteration of the experiment moved the featured resource, which tied into the broadcast schedule, in line with the actual broadcasts. Our hypothesis was that in doing so, it would be a more natural progression to donate and get the book that was referenced in the broadcast.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the layout of the website for potential donors significantly increased the number of clicks. By moving the featured resource to align with the actual broadcasts, visitors were more likely to engage with the content and take action such as donating or obtaining the referenced book.

However, even though the treatment version had more clicks, it had a 47% decrease in conversion rates (with a 47% level of confidence). This, combined with an increase in time spent on page on the treatment of 15%, meant that the placement of the featured resource in line with the broadcasts had the opposite effect of our hypothesis.

experiments.title: How do Meta Lead Ads compare to typical content offer funnels
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by trying out new forms of acquisition for paid media audiences will achieve better acquisition rates because we are staying on the cutting edge of trends.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 05/31/2023
PTV is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
In an effort to find new way to acquire cheaper names and donors, we decided to test Meta Lead Ads for PTV’s top performing content offer.
In the normal funnel set up, a user clicks the Facebook ad, is directed to a landing page on the orgs website where they are asked to complete a form of First Name, Last Name and Email. Once the form is completed the visitor will receive the offer in their inbox and they are redirected to a donation page where they can make a gift to ensure more resources are produced and made available.
In the Meta Lead Ad version, users never leave the Facebook app. The form is completed in the app and the funnel ends with an option to click a button to learn more about the org and therefore make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment showed a 56% increase in the number of names acquired for this offer at a 100% level of confidence.

While this method would show great potential as a method for new name acquisition, our ultimate goal is to acquire donors. We conducted this experiment across multiple and for all offers we saw a valid decrease in donations anywhere from 50-75%.

Due to this, we decided to stick with the traditional funnel and optimize it to get the best results we can.

experiments.title: How a countdown timer impacts donations on a CYE donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a countdown timer on the donation page for email subscribers will achieve an increase in donation because the timer represents urgency to the deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s calendar year-end campaign, we tested the countdown timer feature provided by the donation platform Anedot.
For this test, we specifically targeted email subscribers.
Our hypothesis is that the timer would help to drive urgency and therefore increase donations to the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for the Pathway to Victory year-end campaign aimed to test the effectiveness of adding a countdown timer on the donation page for email subscribers to increase donations. The hypothesis was that the timer would create a sense of urgency and lead to a higher donation rate.
However, after running the experiment for 1 day, it was found that the treatment (with the countdown timer) experienced a 42.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. The results were statistically significant at a 97.3% confidence level, indicating that the control group performed better.
Key Learnings:
1. **Countdown Timer Decreased Transactions:** Contrary to the hypothesis, the presence of a countdown timer did not increase donations, but instead led to a decrease in transactions. This suggests that the timer may have deterred potential donors or created a negative impact on the donation process.
2. **Importance of Testing:** The results highlight the importance of conducting experiments and A/B testing before implementing changes, even if the changes seem promising. In this case, testing revealed that the countdown timer did not have the intended effect.
3. **Understand Audience Preferences:** It is crucial to understand the preferences and behaviors of the target audience before implementing changes. In this case, the audience of Pathway to Victory may not have responded positively to the countdown timer approach.
4. **Optimize Messaging:** While urgency can be a powerful motivator for donations, the manner in which it is communicated is important. Future experimentation should focus on optimizing messaging to create urgency without detracting from the donation experience.
How to Use What Was Learned:
1. **Iterate and Test:** Use the learnings from this experiment to iterate on future campaigns and continue testing different strategies. Experiment with variations in messaging, design, and calls to action to find what resonates best with the audience.
2. **Collect Feedback:** Gather feedback from donors and subscribers to understand their preferences and solicit suggestions for improvement. Implement feedback loops to continuously refine donation strategies.
3. **Monitor Performance:** Keep a close eye on donation metrics and performance indicators to quickly identify any negative trends. Adjust strategies accordingly to optimize donation outcomes.
Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that while the addition of a countdown timer did not yield the desired results, it provided valuable insights that can be used to refine future experimentation and donation strategies for Pathway to Victory.

3. Future experiments should consider the potential impact on donations before making changes to established strategies like the match campaign.

experiments.title: How changing the tone and structure of an email impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the tone and structure for email subscribers will achieve an increase in engagement because the content is present more encouraging and easier to read.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/12/2024
PTV has seen a decrease in performance for this specific offer so we decided to take a radical approach to address a few different opportunities.
Key Differences Between Versions
Tone and Phrasing:
Email A has a more direct and serious tone, emphasizing the certainty of heaven as a “real, tangible place.”
Email B uses a slightly more conversational tone with phrases like, “Have you ever wondered…” and “It’s a question that many of us ponder.”
Positioning of Benefits:
Email A frames the book as a resource to “encourage you and help you prepare for the ultimate destination.”
Email B frames the book as one that will “fill you with hope, curiosity, and confidence.”
Call to Action Language:
Email A says, “I’d love to send you a copy.”
Email B says, “I’d be honored to send you a copy.”
P.S. Section Structure and Content:
The P.S. sections in both emails include different phrasing and lists of additional resources, potentially impacting donor interest.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the tone, phrasing, positioning of benefits, and call to action language in email communications can have a significant impact on donor engagement. The treatment email, with its more conversational tone and positive framing of benefits, resulted in a 66.2% increase in revenue for all traffic compared to the control email. Although the statistical confidence level of 95.0% was not reached, the results are promising and suggest that further testing should be done to validate the findings.

experiments.title: How Facebook copy focused on the felt need met by an offer impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the felt need of Pastors Pen for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher engagement because there is a personal connection of what I could get out of Pastors Pen.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Via direct mail, Pathway to Victory offers a regular mailing of “Pastor’s Pen” a monthly mailout of encouragement from Dr. Robert Jeffress. This piece via direct mail has performed well in being a favorite piece to the audience but also as a channel for revenue.
In an effort to increase channel crossover between digital and direct mail, we decided to try out offering Pastors Pen through Facebook ads where the viewer can sign up to receive Pastors Pen in the mail.
For this offer, we decided to test copy. The control version focuses on the letter being a personal letter from Dr. Jeffress. Whereas the treatment presents Pastors Pen as an encouraging offer when life is difficult.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the perceived benefit or felt need of a product or service can significantly impact engagement and conversion rates. In this case, highlighting how Pastors Pen can be an encouraging resource during difficult times led to a 16.8% increase in clicks through Facebook ads.

While the treatment saw more clicks we actually did not experience an increase in form fills with a 2% increase with 12% loc.

However with donations we saw a 60% decrease with 89% loc.

experiments.title: How providing additional value prop on a content offer impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more value prop in the ad copy for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is additional appeal and clarity on the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV utilizes Facebook as a platform to acquire new names and donors for their organization.
One offer that has been a consistently well-performing content offer is their Jesus Map digital download.
The current version of the ad has been running for multiple years and had not been optimized.
We decided to test out expanding the copy so that there is more information regarding the offer and the reasons to request it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Including more value proposition in the ad copy is effective**: The treatment with expanded ad copy led to a significant increase in Complete Registrations, Clicks, and improvements in Cost per Click and Cost per Registration. This indicates that providing more information and clarity on the offer can attract more viewers and increase conversions.

2. **Audience segmentation is crucial**: The experiment revealed significant increases in Complete Registrations across different visitor segments, such as 65+ visitors, Female visitors, Mobile visitors, and Tablet visitors. This highlights the importance of tailoring messaging and targeting specific audience segments to maximize engagement and conversions.

3. **Statistical significance and confidence levels**: The high confidence levels, especially at 100% for Clicks, Cost per Click, and Cost per Registration, indicate that the results are reliable and not due to chance. This reinforces the validity and impact of the experiment's findings.

To apply these learnings in future experimentation, we should:

1. **Continue to test and optimize ad copy**: Based on the success of the expanded ad copy in this experiment, we should continue exploring ways to enhance value proposition and clarity in our messaging. This could involve testing different variations of ad copy to identify the most effective messaging strategies.

2. **Segment and target specific audience groups**: Building on the positive outcomes seen across different visitor segments, we should further analyze audience data to understand preferences and behaviors. By tailoring content and targeting specific groups, we can drive higher engagement and conversions.

3. **Maintain statistical rigor in experimentation**: To ensure the reliability of results, we should continue to prioritize statistical significance and confidence levels in future experiments. This will help us make data-driven decisions and confidently implement successful strategies.

Overall, by leveraging the learnings from this experiment, we can optimize our digital marketing efforts, enhance audience engagement, and ultimately drive greater success in acquiring new names and donors for Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How does testing donor lookalike audience from HubSpot versus AVID Pathways compare
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using AVID Pathways connection to create donor lookalike audiences for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate in donations because AVID has more data points on donor and therefore can create better lookalike audiences.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/23/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Historically, PTV uses HubSpot’s integration with Facebook to push HS lists into FB and create audiences. While HubSpot is a fantastic platform and has greatly increased the overall performance of PTV’s program, HS only has contacts with email addresses. This means we are unable to target contacts with no email addresses on file.
Recently, PTV signed up for AVID Pathways. AVID has the ability to pull in data from many, many different platforms and marry it all together. This means AVID has access to all donor data regardless of if they have an email or not on file. AVID Pathways is the method in which AVID is able to take the mass amount of data and create segments that can then be pushed into multiple systems, such as Facebook.
We decided to test the HubSpot Facebook integration versus the AVID Pathways Facebook integration to see which platform would convert the most donors. And to make things more interesting, we decided to do this through having Facebook create lookalike audience from HS’s connection and AVID’s connection.
PTV has a popular campaign promoting its book “Perfect Ending” therefore we took this offer and created 2 campaigns. For the control, we took HubSpot’s list of donors, pushed it into Facebook and created a 1% lookalike audience in Facebook. For the treatment, we took the AVID Pathway’s list of donors, pushed it into Facebook and created a 1% lookalike audience in Facebook.
Each variant used the same ad copy and creative, the only difference was the targeting efforts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that AVID Pathways saw a 143% increase in donations with a 99.8% level of confidence.
In additional we found that donors acquired from HubSpot cost $59.83 whereas AVID Pathway donors cost $27.48, this is a 54% decrease in cost with a 99% level of confidence.
With this information, PTV has updated all Facebook campaigns to utilize the AVID Pathways connection. We anticipate results to vary across segments and offers but we expect them to be positive changes.
In addition, just in this one test, a $500/mo. (max) AVID Pathway service saved PTV over $1K on acquisition.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing the fair market value for donation page viewers will achieve a higher average gift because the perceived value of the premium is not influenced by a low fair market value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 12/14/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV uses Anedot as its donation page platform. A feature of the donation page is to show the market value of a premium. When a donation is made, and a premium is given, the tax-deductible amount is the gift amount with the fair market value (FMV) of the premium removed.
PTV frequently self-publishes books as premiums, and therefore, the FMV is typically less than $5. We have observed that the average gift for these premiums is less than professionally published premiums and, therefore, has a more expensive FMV.
We hypothesize that having the fair market value for the premium on the donation is causing the average gift to be lower for self published books because the value of the premium appears to be less.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the fair market value (FMV) for premiums on the donation page significantly increased the average gift and the number of transactions. The treatment group, which had the FMV removed, experienced a 23.2% increase in transactions for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 96.3%.
From these findings, it can be concluded that providing the FMV for premiums on the donation page can negatively impact the perceived value of the premium, leading to lower average gifts and fewer transactions.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue with the approach of not displaying the FMV for premiums. This can help maximize the average gift and increase the number of transactions, ultimately leading to higher overall donations. It may also be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the design and layout of the donation page, to further optimize the donation process.

experiments.title: How sending a follow up email impacted revenue from a text campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by sending a following email for text message recipients will achieve a higher conversion rate  because there is an additional touchpoint through another personal channel.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/26/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
In promoting a new book, we decided to test a follow-up email to half of the audience that received a text message.
For PTV, they rarely use text messages due to cost, and they never do it to the full file, they pick and choose select segments depending on offer, time of the year, and segment that needs extra activation. There are several reasons why we wanted to test this approach.
For one, following up with email, a known and regularly used channel, can help verify that the text wasn’t spam, as that is popular.
Second, if you have multiple channels that can be personalized and timed accurately, why not have those channels work together and support one another? And not in a just more than one is better. But the email called out the text. They are directly connected and not just random that someone received two different things from PTV.
In the case of PTV specifically, they typically suppress text recipients from receiving emails that week so as not to steal results from text. But we are able to show that that isn’t an effective strategy; it’s actually better for channels to play together than being so decisive and siloed
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that sending a follow-up email to text message recipients can significantly increase the conversion rate and revenue generated. The treatment group, which received both a text message and a follow-up email, saw an 80.1% increase in revenue compared to the control group that only received a text message.
This outcome suggests that adding an additional touchpoint through another personal channel, such as email, can be highly effective in capturing the attention and engagement of recipients. It is likely that the email served as a reminder or reinforcement of the message conveyed in the initial text message, leading to a higher conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue using the strategy of sending follow-up emails to text message recipients. This can be implemented as a standard practice, potentially leading to consistent improvements in conversion rates and revenue. Additionally, it may be worth exploring the specific content and timing of the follow-up emails to optimize their effectiveness.
Overall, the success of this experiment reinforces the importance of a multi-channel approach in engaging with audiences and increasing conversion rates. By utilizing multiple channels and touchpoints, organizations can enhance their messaging and communication strategies, leading to improved outcomes and revenue generation.

experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment confirmed that the traditional ad with copy about message and sermon series performed better than more of an invitation to join iCampus. While using more of an invitation to join iCampus was unsuccessful in achieving a higher ad click, this insight can be valuable and instruct how we approach similar issues in the future. A clear takeaway would be to focus advertisement around the message, experience and energy within the church, rather than promoting direct digital connection via iCampus, as the traditional ad was much more successful in driving results. Ultimately, the key learning is that the content of the message matters a great deal when marketing faith-based institutions. Therefore, when creating ads for a similar type of nonprofit, the effectiveness of the ad should be centered around the message rather than a platform for connection.

experiments.title: How urgency language impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which element of urgency will drive response limited time or few left in stock?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/16/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Each year PTV releases a new daily devotional book with 260 days of encouragement and teachings.
To boost response in the final days of the devotional being offered we decided to test whether an urgency of time or an urgency of exclusivity would drive results on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment provided key learnings around the use of urgency to drive response on a Facebook campaign. The results showed that creating a sense of exclusivity, or creating an urgency of few left in stock, yielded 30.2 % more clicks compared to the control which only used an urgency of time. This illustrates the value of using scarcity when crafting a message on a digital campaign. Going forward, creating a sense of exclusivity, combined with a sense of limited time can be used across all types of marketing campaigns to further drive response. If a campaign is not time-sensitive, encouraging users to act quickly due to limited availability can be an effective way to increase response rates.
In addition to driving a click response, we saw a significant impact on donations. The few left in stock generated a 49.6% increase in donor conversions with an 87.1% level of confidence (13 to 75 donations)!
For revenue, we saw a 22.6% increase with a 70.9% level of confidence.
While donations and revenue did not yield confidence levels over the required 95%, we did see directional lifts in a positive direction.

With the average gift for the $10 ask being $33.25 and the average gift for the $15 ask at $35.21.
What remained positive is the trend of not having gifts less than $5. When we tested this back in September with "generous gift" vs $10 we saw that the $10 version had fewer "low ball" donations as people met the goal of the amount presented or even exceeded.

experiments.title: How including an image of the non-profit leader impacts donations in email prospecting
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of the non-profit leader will increase donations by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/20/2023
We referenced the following experiment for a public policy client and utilized it for a faith-based client. Since the results of the past experience were affirmative, we wanted to test the same experiment for a different client. https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4973
In an effort to see a lift in donations in email prospecting for a faith-based client, we wanted to measure the effect of adding credibility and interest to the offer by means of utilizing an image of the non-profit leader.
The offer deployed a daily devotional direct appeal. The control version used the cover image of the daily devotional, while the treatment version utilized the image of the non-profit leader.
This test was set up in which ~50% of the audience would get the control version and ~50% of the audience would get the treatment version. However, once results were clear to email brokers they only deployed the winning version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 91% decrease in donations (with a 99.9% level of confidence) on the treatment creative, we are able to conclude with clarifying results that our experiment hypothesis for this particular client did not match the results of a similar experiment for a different client. We believe that adding an image of the non-profit leader will increase donations by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
The execution of the past experiment was quite similar to this one: We replaced the image of a book/offer premium with the image of the non-profit leader. However, the results were quite different.
It is of importance to note that for this particular client, all other channels tested out of utilizing the image of the leader’s image. We can conclude that the channel email prospecting is no different.

experiments.title: How decreasing the ask amount on a content offer instant donation page impacted conversions (physical resource)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the gift array  for new names acquired will achieve an increase in transactions because we have decreased the perceived sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 10/15/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that has physical resources to acquire new names through Facebook and website traffic.
When a resource is requested, the individual completes a form to receive the item and is then directed to an instant donation page.
The gift array used on these instant donation pages was $25, $50, $75, $95.
As these are brand new people to Pathway to Victory, we wanted to see if reducing the array would increase motivation to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, while we did see a decrease in average gift, $27.24 vs $16.15, we saw an increase of 130.9% in transactions. This increase in transactions contributed to an increase of overall revenue by 37.8% with a 92% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that by providing a lower ask in the gift array, we reduced anxiety for potential new donors. As new contacts are acquired through these resources, a $25 ask can be perceived as a "risk" whereas a $10 ask is more conservative and less of a risk and sacrifice to new prospective donors.
For these donation pages, PTV does not have a minimum donation amount required therefore they experience many gifts that are $1-5. However, with this experiment, we saw a DECREASE in these low-level gifts and saw more donors willing to make a $10 gift.
Our hypothesis is that by having the ask as $10 and "risk" being decreased, then more individuals were willing to make the full donation of $10 or more rather than a donation of $1-5.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 37.8% increase with a 92% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New visitors had a 68.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 194.7% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had a 425.2% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.65+ visitors had a 118.3% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.45-54 visitors had a 79.6% decrease in donations with a 90% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 1,253.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using more personalized copy increases click through rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing copy for the email audience will achieve a higher response rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2022 - 05/06/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry whose donor base has been built on premium offers–books, DVD series, etc. Most of their email file has been accustomed to receiving graphic-heavy emails, with images of the book, etc. For the last of their April 2022 How Can I Know premium offer, we wanted to test whether a more standard, graphic-heavy email would perform better in terms of click throughs and donations compared to a highly personalized, text-only email that did not heavily focus on the book. We only sent this email to those that had not purchased How Can I Know in April.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were interesting, as we had tested a graphic versus plain-text email earlier in the campaign, with the plain-text version generating a higher click-through rate. In this case, the graphic version had a 40% higher click-through rate. However, the results of the test when looking at donations is flipped: the plain-text, personalized copy version had a 83% higher donor conversion rate than the graphic version did, with a 98% level of confidence in test results.

We believe that this is due to the way the copy was written for the plain-text version; it was a much more humanized approach, diving deeper into the questions asked and answered by the book. The graphic version of the email was written more as a purely promotional vehicle for the book itself. Therefore, while less people clicked through to the donation page for the plain-text version, those that did were more motivated to actually complete their donation when they got there.

We will definitely continue this kind of testing for premium offers moving forward for Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How a lesser ask impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking nondonors to give a lesser amount, they will be more likely to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2022
Pathway to Victory promotes a premium each month through its website, tv, radio, and email. The goal is to get resources to strengthen one’s faith and equip one to pierce a dark and fallen culture. But also to raise revenue to keep PTV on the air and expand worldwide.
In the last several months PTV has seen a decline in new donor conversions and overall revenue. This led us to test asking for a lower amount for the promoted premium.
The control group was asked for a gift of $25 or more, while the treatment was asked for a gift of $10 or more.
We kept this test segmented only to PTV nondonors that are on their email list.
We conducted this test over the course of 3 emails (email 1, resend of email 1 to those that did not click the link, and email 2) while keeping the control and treatment audiences separated. And suppressing those that made a gift with each send.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not only did this test increase the average gift by 24.7% we observed the donors that received the $10 ask were more likely to make a larger gift.
Break down of actual gift amounts from the $25 ask
2 - $11 - $51 - $89 - $101 - $2018 - $251 - $352 - $502 - $100
Break down of actual gift amounts from the $10 ask
1 - $22 - $532 - $106 - $155 - $2013 - $251 -  $303 - $502 - $754 - $1001 - $2001 - $400
Since PTV premiums can be acquired with a gift of any amount, they have struggled with donors that give very low $1-5 gifts for the premium. With this test, we not only saw an increase in donations for those that received the $10 ask, we saw a decrease in the very low gift amounts compared to the $25 ask.
Our hypothesis is that when asked for a $10 donation, this ask was low enough that the very low gift donors decided to give the full ask. For an individual donor, the ask for $10 and meeting it when they were expecting to make a low donation is a small sacrifice. But this pays off greatly for the organization when so many people are able to make this small step up from $1-5 to $10.

Timeframe: 05/03/2022 - 05/31/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.

experiments.title: How using value prop language in an email impacted click-through and donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop language for an email audience will achieve a higher click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2022 - 05/24/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of the language used in email appeals has traditionally been focused on the premium offer itself, with mentions of the book, what it contains, and different ways to get the book. For this experiment we wanted to test whether making the email more value-prop focused, with a reference to the book, would impact click-through and donor conversion rates.
This test was run with multiple emails; the results in this experiment are for the 1st email send, including a resend. Both emails pointed to the exact same landing page, which focused on the premium offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were extremely interesting. While there was a 106% increase in click-through rates total, with a 100% level of confidence, when looking at the experiment through a donation conversion lens, the results were different.
When looking at open-to-donation conversion rates, there was a 29% lift in donation conversion rates with the value prop version, with a 70% level of confidence. However, when looking at how many people clicked a link and actually completed the donation, there was a 37% DECREASE in donation conversions, with a 92% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that this result is that while the value prop version was driving more clicks to the landing page, without having any value prop language on the page itself, those potential donors lost their interest in making a donation and receiving the premium.
This then led to the next round of testing, which incorporated value prop language onto the landing page itself.

experiments.title: How placing a premium offer higher on the homepage affected donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by bringing the premium offer higher on the homepage for web viewers that we will achieve increased donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2022 - 05/04/2022
Pathway to Victory (PTV) is a ministry with a donor base built on premium offers that are publicized on both offline (TV, radio, etc) and online channels. Each month a new premium offer is promoted as a resource to donors when they make a donation to the organization. As this is the main source of revenue for PTV, we wanted to test bringing the premium higher on the homepage so that it is above the fold and one of the first things viewers see.
All PTV promotions (direct mail, TV, radio, email, paid, etc.) point to the same premium each month. So when someone visits the PTV website, they are likely, in search for that specific premium, book or bundle.
By moving the offer higher on the homepage, we hypothesise that more people will make a donation for the book as it is easier to locate and learn more. We also added additional information about the specific premium being offered so that there is more clarity around the premium and what is being offered.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By bringing the premium higher on the homepage, and thereby removing friction to getting to the month's featured premium, we saw a 17.2% increase in donations. With making the premium more visible and easily accessible to viewers, more donors were likely to take advantage of the premium offer.
Since we saw positive results with this experiment, we are going to run it again when a premium releases to ensure that we can get repeatable results. If results stay positive, we will move forward with always having the premium in the higher spot.
Future experiment ideas, related to this one, includes making the homepage banner image only about the premium and adding an exit intent popup that highlights that month's premium offer.

experiments.title: How asking for less than a donor’s last gift impacts emails donations for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking a donor for less than their last gift for an email audience will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/08/2022 - 04/30/2022
As part of their April campaign, Pathway to Victory wanted to test a relatively new concept known as the downgraded ask. For this experiment, they created a chart of standards based upon the donors’ previous highest contribution where they would ask for a certain number of donors to give a specific amount. For the treatment version of this ask, they asked for a level below what the donor had previously given and added language acknowledging that they were being asked for less.
This was then tested across an email campaign that included 3 separate emails, with a resend 3 days after the initial send of each.
Pathway to Victory had also never tested included a donors’ previous last gift, so this was a completely new approach for their email file.
We tested this only on the donor file, with the rest of the file receiving the control version that did not have a downgrade ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From a donor conversion standpoint, there is a 70% increase in conversion rates. When looking at the results from a revenue perspective, the positive trend continues, with a 85% increase in revenue per visitor and a level of confidence of 100% due to the larger number of gifts made to the treatment version. There was also a 26% increase in click-through rates a 100% level of confidence. And finally, of those that clicked the links to donate, 35% of them were more likely to convert to donors with the downgrade treatment version.
With the current economic climate (inflation at an all time high), positioning the downgrade ask with a reference to that climate, as well as combining it with a number of donors needed at a given giving level, is helping to drive a more positive response to the treatment version, as well as create a sense of community that is banding together to help provide support to Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How removing friction from a monthly donor landing page increased donor conversions for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction  for prospective monthly donors will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2022 - 04/25/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry with a donor base built on premium offers that are publicized on both offline (TV, radio, etc) and online channels. They have developed a monthly donor program called Pathway Partner, which has a variety of levels and ways to become a Partner. On the control version, there are 12 different combinations of ways you can qualify to become a Partner; we wanted to test whether reducing the amount of friction on that landing page increased monthly donor conversion.
Instead of giving donors the choice between three ‘circles’, we looked at the data and reduced the choice to a single circle level. We also tested updating the design of the landing page, including adding copy at the top that explained the benefits of the program.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By reducing the complication on the page, and providing a single path for potential monthly donors to take to become a Pathway Partner, the donor conversion rate increased by 146%.
The design of the page--less image heavy, with copy focused on why someone should become a monthly donor, the impact they could make, and not just what they will receive as part of the program--was more conducive to monthly donor recruitment. Moreover, while new visitors to the page had a 107 increase in conversions, those that were returning visitors--and therefore had already looked at the various premium offers--had a 343% increase in conversion rates. As this segment of visitors had already been exposed to the impact supporting Pathway to Victory could make, they were much more likely to become a monthly donor and have their impact go 12x as far.

experiments.title: How does adding the number of downloads as an incentive for a content offer impacts emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the number of downloads of the content offer for paid media audiences will achieve an increase in emails acquired because the number would reinforce credibility and incentives since it is so highly requested.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV offers numerous digital download content offers for free as a method to acquire new email addresses that can be cultivated into potential donors.
We decided to test PTV’s best-performing content offer, The Jesus Map, to share the number of downloads for the offer. We hypothesize that this number would help to add credibility and incentive as over 19,000 people had downloaded it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding the number of downloads of the content offer for paid media audiences led to a 17.0% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. This indicates that including social proof, such as the number of downloads, can potentially enhance credibility and incentivize users to engage.

2. Despite the positive impact on email acquisition, the statistical level of confidence was only 83.0%, falling below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered truly reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

3. On the other hand, the experiment also revealed a significant 763% decrease in transactions with a high level of confidence at 96%. This unexpected outcome suggests that while the social proof may have driven more email acquisitions, it could have potentially deterred conversions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider the following implications for future experimentation:

1. While social proof can be a powerful tool in driving user engagement, the impact on other metrics such as transactions should be carefully monitored. It is crucial to balance the benefits of increased credibility with potential drawbacks on conversion rates.

2. Future experiments should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence to ensure the reliability of the results. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or refining the data collection process.

3. It is essential to analyze the overall impact of any changes on multiple metrics to gain a comprehensive understanding of the experiment's effects. This will help in making informed decisions and avoiding unintended consequences.

In conclusion, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of carefully evaluating the trade-offs between different metrics when implementing changes. By considering these learnings and conducting future experiments with a more rigorous approach, we can better understand the impact of our interventions and make data-driven decisions to optimize our strategies.

experiments.title: Will a missional ask or a premium offer perform better as a text message

Each June and December, PTV does a match campaign to encourage donors to give as their impact is doubled.

In email we have seen success in testing the premium book vs a missional ask, and seeing the missional ask prevail as winner.
Therefore we decided to test and see if the same would be true through a text message.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a missional ask in a text message resulted in a 42.2% increase in revenue and 40% increase in donations for Pathway to Victory. This indicates that donors who received a message focused on impacting and supporting the mission of PTV were more likely to donate compared to those who were motivated by receiving a premium book.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of missional asks in different communication channels to see if the trend of higher donor conversion rates holds true across various platforms. Additionally, conducting more experiments with a larger sample size may help to increase the level of statistical confidence and provide more conclusive results.

Overall, the results suggest that emphasizing the mission and impact of Pathway to Victory in donation appeals may be more compelling to donors than offering incentives or premium items. It would be valuable to incorporate this learning into future fundraising campaigns to potentially increase donor engagement and support for the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How including questions to increase relevance impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using conversational “you-focused” language for appeal email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them. because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them. because the reader will have increased curiosity about the personal relevance of the message for them.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/02/2024
When sending an appeal email to the email file, we wanted to find out if using a conversational approach and including two-way communication would improve overall engagement. Rather than just telling the reader what to do and what to read, we wondered if adding relevance, credibility and opening a loop of curiosity will improve the conversion rate by using conversational language. We developed a treatment version of the email pointing to the same donation link, but introduced a series of questions to the reader throughout the message to attempt to pull the reader in and asking them questions to get them thinking and interested in the rest of the email content. We split the message audience 50/50 and measured results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment using conversational language and two-way communication in the email did not lead to the desired increase in donations. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 43.3% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 84.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the balance between personalization and effectiveness of the messaging. While using conversational language and asking questions may increase engagement in some cases, it is crucial to carefully test and analyze the impact of these strategies before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence is essential for making informed decisions based on the results of an experiment. This experiment highlights the importance of thorough testing and cautious interpretation of results before making changes to email communication strategies.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of mentioning previous donations on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test this strategy with different messaging or timing to see if it can yield positive results. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically valid before drawing conclusions. Further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy and determine the best approach to increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Does running brand ads impact CYE donations on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Daily Devotional 2024 CYE 2023 - treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by running brand ads for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there will be brand awareness and knowledge of who PTV is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
December is always a key month for nonprofit fundraising. PTV is no different. Every December, PTV offers a Daily Devotional for the next year as well as a matching challenge.
For this year, on Facebook, we decided to run brand ads to see if this would increase the number of donations.
For the set up, we did 2 campaigns in Facebook.
The control ran Dec 1-31 with ads promoting the Daily Devotional and matching challenge.
The treatment ran Dec 1-15 with brand ads (no ask) just ads about PTV optimized for reach. Then from Dec 16-31 this campaign ran the same Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Brand awareness can positively impact conversion rates:** The treatment group, which focused on brand ads rather than direct ask ads, saw a 16.1% increase in Purchases for All Traffic. This indicates that building brand awareness through ads can lead to higher conversion rates.

2. **Variations in different metrics:** Clicks experienced a significant decrease, while Complete Registrations saw a substantial increase during the treatment phase. Cost per Click, Cost per Registration, and Cost per Purchase also showed notable changes. Additionally, there were decreases in purchases across different visitor types (65+, Mobile, Tablet, Desktop, Female, and Male visitors).

3. **Statistical confidence levels:** While the changes in metrics were significant, the statistical confidence levels were not high enough (85% or above) to consider the results valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Consider longer test durations:** Running experiments for a longer duration may help in achieving higher statistical confidence levels, providing more reliable results for decision-making.

2. **Diversify test approaches:** While brand awareness seems to have a positive impact on conversion rates, it is essential to continue testing different strategies to find the most effective approach for fundraising campaigns.

3. **Segmentation analysis:** Understanding the impact of different visitor types on conversion rates can help tailor campaigns to specific audiences, potentially increasing overall effectiveness.

Overall, while the experiment showed promising results in terms of the impact of brand ads on conversion rates, it is crucial to conduct further analysis and experiments with higher statistical confidence levels before making significant decisions based on these findings.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that running brand ads on Facebook can potentially lead to a higher conversion rate in terms of donations for a nonprofit like Pathway to Victory. The treatment group, which was exposed to brand ads before the Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads, saw a 16.1% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be relied upon for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we will test running brand ads alongside offer ads rather than only brand at the beginning.

experiments.title: How linking straight to a donation page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by going directly to the donation page for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate in gifts because it is a single step process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV shared a book of the month. The process to receive the book is for the email subscriber to make a donation in order to receive the book.
We observed that a majority of email subscribers were making donations in the “generous gift” level therefore we decided to test by skipping the landing page where they choose the level to going straight to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that skipping the landing page and going directly to the donation page for email subscribers resulted in a higher conversion rate in donations. The treatment group saw a 23.2% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, it should be noted that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 81.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered definitive or used for future decision-making.
The average gift for the treatment did decrease by 25% due to the lack of large-level gifts, as the option is not presented on the donation page, only on the landing page.

experiments.title: How tying claims of value to a match impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by expounding the claim of value and tie to the match for email subscriebrs will achieve an increase in donation conversions because the value prop and impact of the match is more clearly explained.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/29/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During December, PTV has a $500k match available to increase motivation for CYE gifts.
In an effort to increase conversions further, we decided to test listing out claims of value for PTV and tie them directly to the match as a method to increase impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Expounding the claim of value and tie to the match for email subscribers can potentially lead to an increase in donation conversions.
2. The treatment group showed a 22.2% increase in donations, which is promising, but not statistically significant at the traditional 95% confidence level.
3. The statistical level of confidence reached 91.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity and further testing is recommended to confirm the effect.
4. It is important to continue testing and refining the messaging and value proposition to maximize the impact on donation conversions.
5. Future experiments should consider adjusting variables such as the match amount, the format of the message, and the target audience to further optimize the results.
6. The insights gained from this experiment can serve as a starting point for future experimentation and allow for more informed decision-making in improving donation conversions.
Note: we did not run this experiment based on revenue due to additional large gifts that significantly skewed results due to calendar year end and additional motivation of the match.

experiments.title: How softening the opening of a Facebook ad impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the opening fear line for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate because fear is not always a successful tactic. Does curiosity perform better?.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/17/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV launched a brand-new book “Are We Living in the End Times?” as a response to the war in Israel. In an effort to increase Facebook conversions we decided to test removing the hard opening sentence “With all the chaos in the world recently, you would not be wrong to start worrying that the End Times are right around the corner.”
Our thought was that this opening line was too much of a discouragement to people especially with so many negative things happening in the world. By removing that line, the ad opens with a more curious approach and less fear.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing a fear-based opening line in a Facebook ad resulted in a significant increase in donations. This suggests that curiosity may be a more effective tactic for driving conversions compared to fear. However, the results are not considered fully valid as the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
To further validate these findings and ensure the intended effect, it is recommended to conduct additional testing. This could involve running similar experiments with different variations of ad copy, such as using a different curiosity-based opening line or testing other emotional appeals. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different ad formats, targeting strategies, or visuals on conversion rates.
By continuing to experiment and iterate on ad messaging and strategies, the nonprofit can optimize their Facebook campaigns to achieve higher conversions and ultimately increase fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How an image of the premium impacted revenue for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for existing donors will achieve a higher revenue because their is a visual appeal of the new book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book in the promotion resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence is not quite at the desired 95%, the results are approaching validity, indicating that the image likely had a positive impact on donor response.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of images in promotions to further validate the results. This could involve testing different types of images, such as a picture of the author or a graphic related to the book's content, to see if they have a similar effect on donor response.

Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze other metrics, such as click-through rates and conversion rates, to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of including images in promotions. This will further inform decision-making and provide a more accurate assessment of the effectiveness of different promotional strategies.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that including an image of the book in promotions can have a positive impact on donor response. By continuing to test and refine this strategy, the nonprofit can potentially increase revenue and attract more donors.

experiments.keyLearnings:

The key learning from this experiment is that an exciting creative alone may not necessarily increase conversions, as evidenced by the decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment. The language used in the promotional campaign may also be a factor worthy of testing in future experiments.

Further testing ideas for this offer via email include, specific dollar amount ask, other placement tests and images of the premium in the email copy.

experiments.title: How adding a donate ‘button’ impacts donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button for web visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 04/16/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry that has a fundraising program that is built around premium offers. The main navigation on their homepage does not lend itself easily to a donor finding a way to make a donation; in this test we created a donate ‘button’ and tested it against the regular ‘donate’ link in the header navigation to see whether it would drive a higher conversion rate. Both of the donate links went to a landing page where the donor could choose which premium the wanted to receive as thanks for their donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a slight increase in conversion rates for users seeing the donate button, with a 3.6% lift in conversion rates and an 81% level of confidence. Interestingly, there was a larger increase in conversion rates when looking at returning visitor traffic, with an increase of 6.7% in conversion rates and an 87% level of confidence.
This is most likely due to the nature of web traffic to the Pathway to Victory website, and the path people take to get there. Generally, people get to the website by heading about Pathway to Victory on TV, the radio, or seeing a digital ad for a specific premium offer. They have no real context about what Pathway to Victory does, so they are less likely to make a donation and are more likely to look for the specific premium they wanted to purchase. However, returning visitors already have some context about the work Pathway does, and are more likely to make a donation to support the organization.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.

Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.

The ‘ways to give’ page is a popular page on PTV’s website, and it traditionally offers different methods where supporters of PTV can donate.
In this experiment, we simplified the amount of copy underneath each ‘box’ that promoted different ways to give, with the goal of reducing friction and increasing conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the copy underneath each 'box' on the 'ways to give' page led to a 2.1% increase in transactions for all traffic. While this result shows a positive impact, the statistical level of confidence was only 19.6%, which is too low to consider the results valid for future decision making.

Moreover, the number of clicks on each variant was flat, while time on page was 45% longer on the control version. Despite this longer time spent on page, the conversion rate was less, indicating that people that are visiting that page are already doing so with the intent to donate.

Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
This year PTV wants to optimize their website to encourage more engagement from site visitors.
This page is where people go to listen past sermons and TV broadcasts.
Building upon the initial test of a grid layout, in this version we wanted to test whether moving the positioning of the resource of the month, as well as the station locator, impacted engagement with the page.

The key learnings from this experiment are that moving the positioning of the resource of the month and station locator on the Pathway to Victory website did not have a significant impact on engagement with the page. Although the treatment group saw a 7.3% decrease in clicks for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.3%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.
One of the goals of this experiment was to see if moving the featured resource in-line with the top grid, rather than a big call-out, led to more gifts. Overall, the treatment version had 3 more gifts in total, which is a 32% increase in conversion rates, but only at a 50% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Does adding an image of a direct mail envelope impact donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an envelope image for direct mail donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/27/2025
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
In March, the direct mail team wanted to test sending a new design of an envelope to donors as a remail strategy. We wanted to target this list with a digital test that looked at whether including the picture of the envelope in the email led to higher conversion rates.
Our hypothesis was that because the envelope was so different from what PTV usually uses, and could easily get lost in the mix, adding an image to the email would drive higher response rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the envelope to the email for direct mail donors did not lead to higher conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a 29.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 73.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is also important to note that the true impact of this test will be whether it influenced offline giving at all; however, direct mail results won't be available for another month, so we are only reporting on instant digital transactions here.

This experiment also had a similar result when looking at it from a revenue perspective. The treatment version had 38% less revenue per visitor than the control, with an 87% level of confidence.

For revenue, while $25/month was the amount mentioned in the email. The treatment saw a 43% increase with a 70% level of confidence. This is primarily due to a $50 gift given to the treatment version. Implying that this donor wanted to reach twice as many people mentioned in the email.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration in order to gather more data and achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment and the specific audience targeted.

When designing future experiments, it may be beneficial to test different versions of the value prop and description to see if there is a more effective way to communicate the benefits of the devotional. experiments.title: How does customizing a gift array based on a donor’s giving history impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that customizing the gift array for donors will achieve a higher revenue per donor because the amount is specific to their giving history.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s year-end efforts, we decided to test the gift array on our donation page.
The control gift array for everyone is $25, $50, $100, $200
We used the donor’s HPC (highest previous contribution) or largest gift as the base for the treatment. Then we multiplied the amount by 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.
To execute this experiment
This organization uses HubSpot as its email marketing platform and Anedot as a donation platform, but this experiment is possible with other platforms.
First, we created new calculated contact properties within HubSpot, which took the largest gift amount and multiplied it by 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3. We also had the contact property round up to the nearest dollar so the donor would not see random amounts with cents.
Second, we reviewed Anedot’s documentation on how to pass the custom amounts.
This test can still be conducted for organizations that do not have these platforms.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate the custom amounts and upload them to your email marketing platform as new fields. Then consult your donation platform documentation to see if/how to pass custom amounts.
If your donation platform does not allow custom amounts, you could break this test into buckets based on HPC. For instance, $1-50, $51-100, $101-150, $151-200, etc, or in whatever buckets best fit your organization. Then, you can AB test by sending half of the bucket to the control and half to a different donation page with a gift array that best fits that bucket’s range.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that customizing the gift array for donors based on their giving history can potentially lead to an increase in revenue per donor. In this particular case, the treatment with customized gift amounts based on the donor's highest previous contribution saw a 40.6% increase in revenue for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 79.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
We will be conducting further analysis on this test to see how much more individuals gave based on the gift array.

experiments.title: Do Meta Suggested Image Optimizations really increase engagement?

Purchases saw a 8% increase but only 62% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important for Pathway to Victory to not solely rely on platform suggestions or promises when conducting experiments. experiments.title: How offering a digital version of Is Christianity the Right Religion impacts email acquisition rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a digital download for a Facebook audience will achieve a better email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 05/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.
This is an extension of an experiment we tested with a single premium in April; for this experiment, we were testing the Is Christianity the Right Religion premium resources.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Pathway to Victory was able to 4.8% increase in email conversion rates; however the level of confidence is only 67%. Moreover, the cost to acquire an email address was 6% higher for the digital premium.

We believe this result is due to a combination of factors:
By offering up immediate gratification through a digital download, versus potentially waiting up to a month to receive your resource in the mail, the audience was much more likely to sign up to receive it.
 	By not requiring a full mailing address for the digital version, we removed some friction in the sign-up process.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:
