Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
To increase donations and revenue, we decided to test increasing the perceived security of the donation form for PTV’s 18 Minutes with Jesus book
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we only saw a 4% increase with a 53% confidence level.
Due to the inconclusive results, we are going to test other methods of security such as an animated lock.

experiments.title: How language specific to existing contacts impacted Facebook clicks

rows.name: FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member control
FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by making ad copy specific to FBD contacts for existing contacts on Facebook will achieve a higher conversion in clicks because they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas. because  they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 05/14/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Leading up to Sunday services, First Dallas utilizes Facebook to share what the sermon will be and invites people to join in person.
This campaign also targets existing First Dallas contacts through the HubSpot/Facebook integration. We decided to test and see if copy that is specific to this audience would increase clicks on the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test if making ad copy specific to First Baptist Dallas's existing contacts on Facebook would result in a higher conversion in clicks. The treatment resulted in a 2.4% increase in clicks for all traffic, but with only a 12.2% statistical level of confidence, the results cannot be considered valid.
During the experiment, the connection between HubSpot and Facebook was broken. This led to the experiment not spending its full budget or reaching its highest potential. We will rerun this experiment in the coming weeks to seek valid results.

experiments.title: How offering a premium versus a direct missional gift impacts conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a missional gift for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 04/23/2023
Pathway to Victory is the ministry of First Baptist Dallas, and fundraises through a premium-focused program.
As part of that program, they have a new name acquisition program where people are asked to provide an email address and contact information in exchange for a digital version of a resource offer.
In this experiment, we were testing whether asking people for a gift after that initial sign-up with a missional ask had a better conversion rate than with a resource-based ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a missional gift for potential donors might lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 19.8% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 39.6%, which is not high enough to be considered valid.
Since this is an ongoing acquisition effort, we will let this experiment run until for a bit longer; we will also test this approach on different acquisition offers to see whether it is the actual premium being offered that is helping skew results, or if it is truly missional giving that motivates new names to donate to Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How changing the language for existing donors impacts email conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing a donor’s status for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because we are showing them they are more than just an email address.. because we are showing them they are more than just an email address..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 04/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
One of their best-performing offers is a book titled Perfect Ending; this is an offer they run on multiple channels at least once a year. For this specific email appeal, we wanted to test whether recognizing the impact a donor has had on the ministry by changing the language in an email would impact their conversion rates. Our hypothesis was that by referencing that they were a donor, they would be more likely to make a donation. This test was run for the active donor segment only.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Changing the language in the donation ask slightly, using terms such as 'continue your support' and ' reinforce that you stand behind bold....' resulted in a slight decrease in conversion rates of 8.5% with a 49% level of confidence.

There was a bigger change when looking at the impact on revenue; a 10.9% decrease in revenue, with a 68% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to the fact that this experiment only changed a couple of words; the call to donate was buried at the bottom of the email, so potential donors to this campaign were already convinced to donate and get a copy of the book before they were even influenced by the donor-specific copy.

Future variants of this test will look at the placement of donor-centric language, as well as bigger copy tests.

experiments.title: How a simplified Facebook image impacts click converstions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by simplifying the image and removing friction  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve higher click conversion because the ad will highlight the important aspects of the sermon.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks, the Facebook ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image that has Dr. Jeffress’s image removed and the sermon title removed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment tested the effectiveness of using an ad with a simplified image and message versus an ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress. The hypothesis was that the simplified ad would achieve higher click conversions by reducing friction. However, the results showed that the control ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress performed better, with a 4.3% increase in clicks for all traffic at a 61.1% level of confidence.
Since the level of confidence is below the 85% threshold, the results are not considered valid, and future decisions should not be based on them. There are some potential insights that can be gleaned from the experiment, such as the effectiveness of including details about the sermon and featuring Dr. Jeffress in the ad. Future experiments could build upon these insights to test different messaging and images to see if they can improve ad performance.

experiments.title: How a simplified image impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the content on the image for Facebook viewers will achieve an increase in clicks because it is less friction and simplified information.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks the Facebook ad performance as been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image of only the sermon series title.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment indicates that simplifying language and images on Facebook ads may not be the best way to achieve increased clicks. Our hypothesis was that by reducing information on the image and content, Facebook viewer engagement would increase. However, as demonstrated in this experiment, clicks were actually 6.1% lower when a simplified image of only the sermon series title was used.
It is important to remember that although the control won in this experiment, the statistical confidence was too low to be considered valid. This means the experiment has not completely invalidated our hypothesis and the test of simpler ad copy and imagery may still be worth experimenting with again in the future.
As a result, the key learning from this experiment is that more experimentation should be done in regards to messaging, images, and ad copy in order to find out what performs best. It is important to conduct experiments often and on a consistent basis in order to stay updated on changes in Facebook ad performance and understand what works best at any given time.

experiments.title: How promoting a specific gift amount impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking existing donors for a specific gift amount for email audience will achieve higher conversion rate because the decision anxiety of how much to donate has been removed.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2023 - 02/08/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas that shares the truth of God’s Word through radio, television, digital resources, and books.
Each month PTV offers a different book resource to their email file that provides the audience with an opportunity to make a donation and receive the resource as an appreciation gift.
Existing donors on the PTV email file historically receive a nonspecific gift amount asked. In an effort to increase donor conversion, we decided to test a specific ask amount for this audience.
We have conducted similar testing for the nondonor email segment and have seen positive results in providing nondonors with a specific gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On the email level, we did not see any significant or valid directional lifts. However, for donations and revenue, we see an interesting story...
For donations, the control saw 15 donations and 16 donations for the treatment. That calculates to a 5.2% increase with an 11.1% level of confidence.
For revenue, the control brought in $975 with an average gift of $65, and the treatment brought in $789 with an average gift of $49.31.
As a reminder, the treatment asked for a donation of $25 or more while the control was nonspecific.
The transaction break down for the two variants were
Control - nonspecific amount ask
1 - $10 gift3 - $25 gifts1 - $30 gift1 - $35 gift3 - $50 gifts1 - $75 gift3 - $100 gifts2 - $150 gifts
Treatment - $25 ask
2 - $7 gifts7 - $25 gifts4 - $50 gifts2 - $75 gifts1 - $250 gift
For revenue we saw a 13.3% decrease in overall revenue with a 61.6% level of confidence
Surprisingly, we saw that the control donors were more likely to make a gift of $25 or more without being prompted with 11 gifts. While the treatment audience was more likely to stay at the $25 level.
As this audience did not respond as well as to the specific ask as the nondonor segment has, we will begin testing into generic language around value prop and mission of PTV to see if a change will increase response.

experiments.title: How a lower monthly ask impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by decreasing the monthly amount to $10 for web traffic will achieve an increase in transactions because the lower amount is more attainable and less of a financial sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 02/04/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Church Dallas. Through radio, tv, digital efforts, and resources Pathway to Victory shares the truth of God’s word with people worldwide.
In January 2023, we emphasized Pathway to Victory’s Pathway Partner program. This is a recurring gift program where the donor makes a monthly or annual gift to PTV and receives resources each month and exclusive access to a library of sermons and other incentives.
Traditionally the entry Pathway Partner monthly amount has been $25. We decided to test an entry of $10 to see if this would increase the number of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall we only saw a 9.4% increase in conversions with a 47.2% level of confidence.
In revenue, we saw a small decrease in the average gift but that is not surprising due to the lower ask of the treatment.
The average PTV audience falls within the 65+ age range, however we saw that 35-44 visitors had a 415.9% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence (1 vs 5 donations). While this was not the purpose of this test, activating a younger audience, it is interesting to see that impact.
We will continue testing into how we can convert more people to become Pathway Partners.

experiments.title: How a email subject line drives clicks

rows.name: 10 Core Beliefs for Standing Strong in a Shifting World
Brand-new book "What Every Christian Should Know"

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding clarity to the subject line for the housefile will achieve an increase in click conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2023 - 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth. In that effort, each month PTV shares a book or resource to equip Christians to share the truth of God.
The premium for January 2023 is a brand-new book by Dr. Robert Jeffress titled “What Every Christian Should Know”.
In an effort to drive premium purchases, we decided to test the subject lines in the initial kick-off email to see which resonated more with the individuals on the email list. For our treatment, we added clarity by announcing the release of the brand-new book, while the control stayed in alignment with the traditional approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this test, we saw 2 different results.
For the "10 core beliefs" subject, we saw a 5.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence in opens. However, we know that this is not a conversion rate that should be totally trusted due to various platforms "pre-opening" emails.
For the "Brand-new book" subject line, we saw a 10% increase in clicks with a level of confidence at 65.4%.
What we found interesting is that more people open the email with the subject line "10 core beliefs" however the higher click count occurred on the "Brand-new book" subject line. This leads us to believe that people are looking for information around the core beliefs but were not as interested in purchasing a book. Whereas the "brand-new book" subject line saw more clicks meaning that people knew what they were opening and were more interested in learning more about the book.
For this new book, we are going to continue testing copy and seeing which angle of this book resonates best in an effort to drive purchases.

experiments.title: How remove Dr. Jeffress’ image impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing Dr. Jeffress’ image from the year-end landing page increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/16/2022
PTV is a broadcast ministry with a premium-based fundraising program.
Dr. Jeffress is the host and preacher for PTV therefore his image is prominent in most promotions, landing pages, and websites.
Though his face is known to his TV audience, his radio, email and newly acquired names may not be familiar with his image.
For the year-end campaign, we decided to test the landing page without his image to see if this impacted donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a statistically valid increase of donations, we also did not see any harm with removing Dr. Jeffress' image.
On mobile, we had expected to see more of an impact of the treatment version. With the control version on mobile, Dr. Jeffress' image takes up the entire screen between the paragraphs of copy and the donation level options. So with the treatment version having the image removed, we expected an improved mobile experience and less friction however the data did not end up supporting that.
We will continue testing on this landing page to improve donor conversion as we are seeing higher than normal web traffic given the year end final push.

experiments.title: How campaign first messaging impacts clicks from donors in Giving Tuesday emails
experiments.researchQuestion: What messaging will get a more positive response for Giving Tuesday in an email campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that does its primary fundraising through monthly premiums. For Giving Tuesday, they offered an exclusive bundle only available on that day.
As Giving Tuesday has grown exponentially around the world, we wanted to test how we could stand out during a small timeframe when all organizations are making an ask.
We tested this by using Giving Tuesday based messaging at the beginning of the email and then later talked about the mission of PTV for the control and developed a treatment using PTV mission-focused messaging at the beginning of the email and then later used Giving Tuesday messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive a sufficient change in clicks, only 2.5% increase with a 16.8% level of confidence. We did see a 9.2% increase in opens with a 99% level of confidence.
While email opens is no longer a reliable metric, due to Apple and other platform privacy rules. We did observe a high enough increase that warranted a deep look.
When the control email hit inboxes, the viewer saw the subject line and the first few words of the email which were "Hello [firstname], On #Giving Tuesday..." For the treatment, the viewer saw the same subject line but a different first few words. They saw "Hello [firstname], As you know Pathway to Victory..."
Our hypothesis is that the increase of opens is due to the initial response of the email not being Giving Tuesday focused like ever other email in the inbox.
As we did not see a valid response to this test, we will continue testing to see if the order of messaging can impact conversion rates.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the ‘I’m new’ button for new website visitors impacted email acquisition rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlight a specific section for new visitors will achieve a change in click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
As one of the largest Baptist churches in the country, First Baptist Dallas drives a lot of new website traffic to it’s site every day. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether highlighting the ‘I’m New’ navigation menu in the header menu for new visitors only made an impact on email sign-ups on that specific page.
Our hypothesis was that by highlighting this section, any new visitor to the site would easily find information that was more relevant to them, instead of spending time searching through the site. New visitors were also asked to fill out a form on that page to receive more information; by reducing the amount of friction to find that form, our hypothesis was that email acquisition rates would be higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Highlight the 'I'm New' header navigation menu for new visitors to the site led to a small, 1.7%, increase in email acquisition rates, with only a 48.8% level of confidence.

While this shows a slight directional lift in email rates, this test warrants further refinement. For example, the form where people are asked to fill in their information is actually a plan your visit (for Sunday service) form; not all of the new visitors to the homepage went there with the intention of attending a Sunday service. The form itself is also at the bottom of the 'I'm New' page; further testing would place this form higher, potentially above the fold, and make it more appealing to new visitors by offering more than just Sunday service information in exchange for an email sign-up.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a premium landing page impacted donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop langauge for premium-resource driven donors will achieve a change in conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Their featured offer in May was Invincible: Conquering the Mountains That Separate You from the Blessed Life.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this specific offer, adding in value proposition language to the landing page for the book led to a decrease in both conversion rates and revenue per donor of 14.2% and 8.3% respectively. Despite an increase in average gift, the overall result was a decline in revenue for the experiment variant page.
Our hypothesis is that the majority of traffic to these book offer pages is driven directly from TV and radio programs promoting the book; those that reach that page want to purchase the book and don't have that much of an interest in value proposition.
However, it will be interesting to keep track of the 'value prop' donors long term, as our hypothesis is that by buying into the value prop of WHY they should donate to PTV instead of another ministry, they will become better long-term donors, and not just those looking to buy one book and that is the extent of their support.

experiments.title: How moving a premium offer above the fold impacted donations for Pathway to Victory

rows.name: Kids book above the fold on website

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving an offer above the fold for website visitors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2022 - 06/06/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. This year’s June match campaign focused on the launch of a brand new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 challenge match.
Traditionally, the homepage of Pathway to Victory only shows an image of the featured monthly premium as a small image above the fold, and a larger focus below the fold. For this experiment, we wanted to see whether moving the image and description of the kids book above the fold would make an impact on donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Moving the image of the new kids book above the fold had a meaningful impact on donor conversion rates and revenue, despite not reaching a 100% level of confidence. The 32.6% increase in donation conversions, with a 78% level of confidence, indicates that placing the image on an area where most of website visitors will see will lead to a change in donations.
Our hypothesis is that because the majority of traffic to the homepage is driven through radio and TV offers where they reference the premium offer, placing the image above the fold made it easier for potential donors to make a donation without having to search for where they can find the book.
Since Pathway to Victory is a premium-focused organization, this is a test we will continue to run with every offer.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar impacts donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2022 - 06/30/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether including a humanized sticky bar on the premium-focused landing page helped drive a higher donor conversion rate.
The sticky bar featured an image of Dr. Jeffress, the senior pastor for Pathway to Victory, and language that focused on the doubled impact a gift would have thanks to the match campaign. Since there were 4 separate ways to get the premium based on giving level, we ran this sticky bar test on all 4 donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: The use of a personalized sticky bar did not have a very large impact on donor conversion rate, with only a 2% lift and a 46% level of confidence. Since the majority of the copy on the landing page was premium-focused, and did not really mention the match campaign, the sticky bar was the only, small, piece of digital real estate where donors could potentially be motivated more to make a gift because of the match itself, and not the premium offer.

experiments.title: How using a match-focused pop-up on the homepage impacted results for Pathway to Victory.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a pop-up to the homepage for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether having a pop-up on the homepage with match copy made an impact on donor conversion rates.
Since the focus of the campaign was the children’s book, there was limited reference to the match campaign on the homepage of the site. This test aimed to see whether adding match language made any impact
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment failed to validate or provide any direction on whether a pop-up on the homepage during a match campaign would impact results. With a slight decrease in conversion rates of 2%, but only 17% level of confidence, this experiment needs further tweaking for future match campaigns.

Our hypothesis on why this had no impact is that the majority of people go to the homepage after watching a Pathway to Victory program on TV, or hearing one of the radio broadcasts. They are not necessarily driven to the site looking for the match campaign; therefore, offering them up language that a match was going on would not motivate them to donate.

experiments.title: How using an exit-intent pop-up during a match campaign impacted conversion results for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an exit intent for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/08/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. They have 2 main match campaigns throughout the year- one in December, and one in June. For this year’s June campaign, which focused around the launch of a brand-new children’s book, coupled with a $500,000 Gospel Advance match, we wanted to test whether including an exit intent pop-up made an impact on conversion rates.
This pop-up was triggered when someone would mouse over the ‘back’ button on their browser; the copy emphasized the impact that making a gift during this limited-time match challenge would have on the ministry.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not reach the level of confidence where we would say it was valid, a 61% level of confidence in the 3.7% increase in donor conversion rates indicates that this was a useful tactic.
Since the donation page itself is very premium-focused with little reference to the actual match campaign, using this exit intent pop-up allowed us extra digital real estate to communicate the impact this campaign would have on the ministry.
This is a tactic we will continue testing during high donation frequency campaigns for this organizations.

experiments.title: How shortening the number of fields for a ‘plan your visit’ form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing form fields for a prospective visitor will achieve  a higher sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 03/31/2022
First Baptist Dallas has a ‘plan your visit’ form where they ask prospective visitors for Sunday’s church services a series of questions. The form had multiple required entries; we wanted to test whether splitting the form into two parts drove a higher conversion rate.
In our treatment version, the first part of the form just had basic questions (name, email), while the second part then asked the rest of the demographic questions that were present on the control form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the number of email signups was the same, the 2-step form had a slightly higher conversion rate than the control version, with a 10% increase in email acquisition.
Since filling out the form was not a requirement for attending church services on Sunday, the number of form fills was not enough to fully validate the experiment. Moreover, there was confusion on the back end with the 2-step form, as they were getting duplicate submissions (one with the name/email, one with the rest of the information asked).
For our next test of this, we will test streamlining the back-end process, as well as making the form fill a required part of church attendance. We would also test incentivizing form fills by offering a premium.

experiments.title: How reducing the amount of information required on an event registration form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing form fill fields for event attendees will achieve a higher conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 04/02/2022
First Baptist Dallas was very excited to announce that Rebecca St. James would be the Worship Resident Artist for their Day One worship service. As part of the landing page providing details on the service, First Baptist included a form that had multiple questions. We wanted to test whether moving most of the questions to a 2nd page would impact form conversion rates. For the treatment, we just asked for first/last/email before directing them to a second form that asked the additional questions included in the control version. Our hypothesis was that by reducing the form-fill friction, prospective attendees would be more likely to fill out the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Reducing the number of fields that a website visitor had to fill out actually reduced the conversion rate by 11% for overall traffic. However, there were not enough conversions to provide a valid test result. Moreover, having two separate forms created some confusion for the staff that was managing entries, as they were receiving double form submission (one with the first/last/email, one with the rest of the information) for the treatment version.
We believe there was a decline in conversion rates as the people potentially attending the Day One service were used to filling out multiple fields from other events, as well as wanted to provide as much information as possible up front. Moreover, since filling out the form was not a requirement to attend, it was easy for potential attendees to just ignore it.
We will continue to test this concept, with future tests focusing on a reduction of friction and labor on the back-end, as well as making it clear what filling out the form would actually achieve.

experiments.title: How shortening the number of fields for a ‘plan your visit’ form impacted conversion rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing form fields for potential visitors will achieve a higher sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/18/2022 - 03/30/2022
First Baptist Dallas has a ‘plan your visit’ form where they ask prospective visitors for Sunday’s church services a series of questions. The form had multiple required entries; we wanted to test whether splitting the form into two parts drove a higher conversion rate. The first part of the form just had basic questions (name, email), while the second part then asked the rest of the demographic questions that were present on the control form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the number of conversions was relatively close, the 2-step form had a 1.7% higher sign-up rate, with 100% level of confidence. This is a test we will continue to run on different forms throughout the website.

experiments.title: How removing images from emails affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing graphics for the digital audience will achieve higher click through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2022 - 01/28/2022
With a file that is accustomed to receiving emails that are very graphic-heavy and premium focused, Pathway to Victory’s file was primed for a plain-text test. For this experiment, we removed all images and added in the full URLs (instead of hyperlinking text). While we had previously tested this during a high-urgency campaign, with the plain-text version winning, we wanted to see whether this trend would continue for a ‘regular’ campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The control version received more clicks than the plain-text test version, but the number of clicks was so close that there was no statistically valid result from this experiment.
